title: Remarks at the Versailles EU Leaders Summit
occasion: Informal EU Heads of State Summit on Ukraine Crisis

paragraphs.text: Your Excellencies, colleagues, dear friends. We gather today at Versailles at a moment of profound gravity for our continent. Two weeks ago, Russia launched an unprovoked and unjustifiable war of aggression against Ukraine, a sovereign European state. The images we see daily — of bombed hospitals, of civilians fleeing under fire, of a democratic nation under siege — are a direct assault on the values upon which our Union was built.

Let me be direct: the invasion of Ukraine has shattered the post-Cold War security architecture of Europe. The assumptions that underpinned decades of European policy — that economic interdependence would prevent conflict, that borders in Europe were settled, that diplomacy would always prevail — these assumptions have been proven dangerously naive. We must now face reality with clear eyes and act accordingly.

Europe's strategic autonomy is no longer an abstract concept debated in think tanks and policy papers. It is an urgent necessity. We cannot continue to depend on others for our security, our energy, and our critical technologies. The question before us today is whether we have the collective will to build a Europe that can defend itself, that can power itself, and that can innovate for itself.

On defense, we must dramatically accelerate our investments. France has already announced a significant increase in its defense budget, and I welcome the historic decision by Germany to establish a special fund of one hundred billion euros for the Bundeswehr. But national efforts alone are not sufficient. We need coordinated European defense procurement, joint capability development, and a genuine European pillar within NATO.

I am proposing today that we establish a European Defense Investment Facility — a mechanism to finance joint defense projects at scale. Cyber defense, space capabilities, autonomous systems, long-range precision strike — these are areas where European cooperation is not just desirable but indispensable. No single European nation can match the investments of the United States, China, or Russia alone. Together, we can.

The energy dimension of this crisis has exposed a vulnerability that we should have addressed long ago. Europe imports forty percent of its natural gas from Russia. This dependency has given Moscow leverage over our foreign policy and constrained our ability to respond to aggression. We must end this dependency, and we must end it rapidly.

France proposes a three-pronged strategy for energy sovereignty. First, immediate diversification of gas supplies through LNG terminals, new pipeline connections, and contracts with alternative suppliers in Norway, Algeria, Qatar, and the United States. Second, massive acceleration of renewable energy deployment — solar, wind, hydrogen — with streamlined permitting processes across the Union. Third, investment in nuclear energy as a stable, low-carbon baseload power source.

I know that nuclear energy remains a subject of debate among our member states, and I respect the choices of those who have taken a different path. But let us be honest: in a world where energy security and climate action must advance simultaneously, we cannot afford to exclude any proven low-carbon technology from our toolkit. The taxonomy debate must reflect this reality.

Beyond energy and defense, this crisis has underscored our dependence on global supply chains for critical raw materials, semiconductors, and pharmaceutical ingredients. The European Chips Act is a start, but we need a broader strategy for economic resilience. We must map our strategic vulnerabilities and develop European production capacities in every critical sector.

Let us also be clear about the economic dimension of our response to Russia. The sanctions we have imposed are unprecedented in their scope and severity. We have frozen the assets of the Russian Central Bank. We have cut major Russian banks from the SWIFT system. We are targeting the oligarchs who sustain the Putin regime. These sanctions are working — the Russian economy is already contracting sharply.

But sanctions must be sustained and, if necessary, strengthened. We must close every loophole, prevent every circumvention, and maintain unity among all twenty-seven member states. I want to commend the extraordinary solidarity we have shown in this crisis. Europe has acted with a speed and decisiveness that few thought possible. We must maintain this unity in the difficult months ahead.

On Ukraine's European aspirations, President Zelensky has submitted Ukraine's application for European Union membership, and I believe we should respond with seriousness and respect. The accession process has its requirements and its timeline, and we must not compromise on our standards. But we should offer Ukraine a concrete and credible path toward European integration, because the Ukrainian people are fighting and dying for the values we share.

At the same time, we must prepare for the humanitarian consequences of this conflict. More than two million Ukrainians have already fled their country. Poland, Romania, Hungary, Slovakia, and Moldova are bearing an enormous burden. Europe must share this responsibility through a coordinated reception mechanism, financial support for frontline states, and a commitment to treat Ukrainian refugees with the dignity they deserve.

I want to address our citizens directly. I know that the consequences of this crisis — rising energy prices, inflation, economic uncertainty — are being felt in households across Europe. These are real hardships, and we must not minimize them. France has implemented an energy price shield, and we are prepared to do more. But I also ask our citizens to understand what is at stake: the fundamental principles of international law, sovereignty, and democracy.

To President Putin, I say again what I have said in our many hours of dialogue: there is no military solution to this conflict. Every day this war continues, more Ukrainian civilians die, more Russian soldiers die, and Russia's isolation deepens. The path to a ceasefire and negotiated settlement exists, but it requires Russia to stop its bombardment, withdraw its forces, and engage in genuine diplomacy.

Colleagues, the decisions we make in the coming weeks and months will shape the future of our continent for decades. We have a choice: we can treat this crisis as a temporary disruption and return to business as usual once the guns fall silent, or we can use this moment as the catalyst for the transformation that Europe has needed for a generation. I believe we must choose transformation.

A sovereign Europe is a Europe that can defend its borders, secure its energy, feed its people, develop its own technologies, and project its values in the world. This is not about building a fortress; it is about building the capacity to act. An open Europe and a strong Europe are not contradictions — they are complements.

I therefore propose that by June, we adopt a comprehensive European Sovereignty Agenda — a roadmap with concrete milestones for defense, energy, digital, and industrial autonomy. Each milestone should have clear timelines, dedicated funding, and accountability mechanisms. The era of grand declarations without follow-through must end.

History is watching us. The people of Ukraine are watching us. Our own citizens are watching us. Let us prove that Europe is not merely a fair-weather project — that when the storm comes, we stand together, we act together, and we emerge stronger together. Thank you.
